item_a.name: Fidelity Investments
item_a.image_alt: Fidelity Investments brokerage platform dashboard showing trading interface and account management
pros: Zero commission trades on stocks and ETFs
Excellent customer service with 24/7 phone support
Strong research tools and educational resources
Wide range of mutual funds with zero expense ratio options
cons: Less advanced charting tools compared to professional platforms
item_a.best_for: Buy-and-hold investors and retirement savers who value customer service and educational resources.

item_b.name: Interactive Brokers
item_b.image_alt: Interactive Brokers Trader Workstation platform displaying advanced trading tools and market data
pros: Extremely low margin rates starting at 5.83%
Best-in-class pricing for active traders with tiered commission structure
cons: Platform can feel overwhelming for casual investors
Monthly inactivity fee of $20 for accounts under $100,000 with less than $10 in commissions
item_b.best_for: Active traders and international investors who need access to global markets and advanced trading tools.

comparison_summary: Fidelity and Interactive Brokers serve distinctly different investor profiles. Fidelity excels as a comprehensive wealth management platform with superior customer service and beginner-friendly tools, while Interactive Brokers dominates in global market access, low-cost margin trading, and professional-grade platforms. Your choice depends primarily on whether you prioritize ease-of-use and support (Fidelity) or advanced features and international access (Interactive Brokers).
verdict_text: Fidelity wins for retirement investors and beginners seeking simplicity, while Interactive Brokers is superior for active traders and those requiring global market access.

key_differences.item_a: $0 for U.S. stocks, ETFs, and options ($0.65 per contract)
key_differences.item_b: $0 for IBKR Lite; $0.0035/share (min $0.35) for IBKR Pro

key_differences.item_a: Limited to U.S. and Canadian securities
Fidelity.com web platform, Active Trader Pro desktop
key_differences.item_b: Trader Workstation (TWS), IBKR Mobile, Client Portal
Advanced analytics tools but fewer third-party reports

key_differences.item_a: No inactivity fees or account minimums
key_differences.item_b: $20/month inactivity fee if commissions under $10 and balance under $100k

seo_content:
When comparing Fidelity vs Interactive Brokers, investors are evaluating two powerhouses that serve fundamentally different needs in the brokerage landscape. Both platforms offer commission-free trading on U.S. stocks and ETFs, but the similarities largely end there.

Fidelity has built its reputation over 75 years as a trusted wealth management partner for millions of Americans. The platform shines for retirement investors, offering exceptional Roth IRA and 401(k) rollover services alongside proprietary mutual funds with zero expense ratios. Fidelity's customer service remains industry-leading, with 24/7 phone access to knowledgeable representatives and over 80 physical branches where you can speak with advisors face-to-face. The platform's educational content is comprehensive, making it ideal for investors still learning the fundamentals of portfolio construction and asset allocation.

Interactive Brokers vs Fidelity becomes a different discussion when you're an active trader or need international exposure. IBKR provides unmatched access to 150+ markets spanning 33 countries, from European exchanges to Asian markets that most U.S. brokers simply don't offer. The margin rates are industry-leading at 5.83% to 6.83%, compared to Fidelity's 8.325% to 9.325%, which makes a substantial difference for traders using leverage. Interactive Brokers' Trader Workstation (TWS) is a professional-grade platform with advanced charting, algorithmic trading capabilities, and sophisticated order types that day traders and options specialists require.

The cost structure reveals important distinctions. While both brokers advertise $0 commissions, Interactive Brokers charges a $20 monthly inactivity fee for accounts under $100,000 that generate less than $10 in commissions. This effectively makes IBKR unsuitable for passive investors with smaller portfolios. Fidelity imposes no such fees, welcoming investors at any activity level.

For options trading, both platforms charge $0.65 per contract, though Interactive Brokers' IBKR Pro tier offers volume discounts that serious options traders will appreciate. Futures traders will find Interactive Brokers far superior, with access to global futures markets and competitive per-contract pricing that Fidelity simply cannot match.

The Fidelity vs Interactive Brokers decision ultimately hinges on your investor profile. Choose Fidelity if you're building long-term wealth through retirement accounts, value accessible customer support, prefer a user-friendly interface, or invest primarily in U.S. markets. Select Interactive Brokers if you're an active trader, need global market access, use margin regularly, trade complex derivatives, or want the absolute lowest costs for high-volume trading. Both are excellent brokers that have earned their reputations—just in very different ways.

faq.q: Which is better for beginners: Fidelity or Interactive Brokers?
faq.a: Fidelity is significantly better for beginners due to its intuitive interface, extensive educational resources, and 24/7 phone support. Interactive Brokers' platforms have a steep learning curve and are designed for experienced traders who need advanced features.

faq.q: Does Interactive Brokers really have lower fees than Fidelity?
faq.a: For active traders using margin or trading internationally, Interactive Brokers offers substantially lower costs with margin rates 2-3% cheaper than Fidelity. However, Fidelity has no account minimums or inactivity fees, making it more cost-effective for casual investors with smaller portfolios.

faq.q: Can I trade international stocks with Fidelity?
faq.a: Fidelity offers limited international trading access, primarily Canadian stocks and some ADRs (American Depositary Receipts). Interactive Brokers provides direct access to 150+ markets in 33 countries, making it the clear choice for investors wanting genuine global diversification.

faq.q: Which broker has better retirement account options?
faq.a: Fidelity excels in retirement accounts with a wider selection of IRA types, seamless 401(k) rollover services, proprietary target-date funds, and dedicated retirement planning tools. While Interactive Brokers offers IRAs, its platform is optimized for active trading rather than long-term retirement planning.

faq.q: Are there any account fees at Fidelity or Interactive Brokers?
faq.a: Fidelity charges no account maintenance, inactivity, or transfer fees. Interactive Brokers charges a $20 monthly inactivity fee for accounts under $100,000 that generate less than $10 in commissions, though this fee is waived for accounts over $100,000 and investors under 25.

meta_title: Fidelity vs Interactive Brokers (2026): Which Broker Should You Choose?
meta_description: Detailed comparison of Fidelity vs Interactive Brokers. We break down commissions, margin rates, platform features, and international access to help you pick the right broker.
